planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded, moist woodland-style bed.

directions: Unusual for the genus, this silver-leaved selection bears white rather than blue flowers in spring. Grow in part to full shade with rich, consistently moist soil, keeping the crown level with the ground. Divide established clumps in early spring or fall to propagate this named form.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade with cool, evenly moist soil.

directions: This green-leaved form is grown for its pure white forget-me-not flowers, a rare color break in the genus. Site it in part to full shade with rich, moisture-retentive soil and plant the crown at ground level. White sprays open in spring above plain green foliage; increase by dividing clumps.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded border with humus-rich, moist soil.

directions: Also known as 'Aluminum Spot', this vigorous selection has green leaves marked with a ring of silvery-gray spots near the margins. Grow in part shade with steady moisture, setting the crown level with the soil. Blue flowers appear in spring, and it is an easy, robust plant to divide in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade; avoid hot sun that scorches the golden leaves.

directions: This selection emerges with bright chartreuse to gold foliage that softens as the season progresses and burns in direct sun. Plant in full or part shade with cool, consistently moist soil and the crown at ground level. Blue flowers open in spring; protect from midday sun and propagate by division to preserve the leaf color.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded bed with rich, evenly moist soil.

directions: Grown for solid green, glossy heart-shaped leaves without silver or variegation, this form makes a lush shade groundcover. Site in part to full shade with steady moisture and plant the crown level with the soil. Sprays of blue spring flowers rise above the foliage; divide clumps in early spring to spread.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade with cool, moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: This selection carries variegation that shifts across the season, with silver frosting and creamy edges giving a mottled, changeable look. Grow in part to full shade with reliable moisture and set the crown at soil level. Blue flowers appear in spring; propagate by division to keep the coloring consistent.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade with room for its large leaves and broad habit.

directions: A large-leaved cousin of 'Jack Frost', this selection makes very big silver-overlaid leaves with heavy substance and a bold clump. Allow generous spacing and grow in part shade with rich, consistently moist soil, keeping the crown level. Blue spring flowers hover above the foliage; divide mature plants to propagate.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded, moisture-retentive bed.

directions: This selection is distinguished by narrower, more pointed silver leaves that give the clump a slightly spear-shaped outline. Grow in part shade with cool, evenly moist soil and plant the crown at ground level. Blue forget-me-not flowers open in spring, and clumps divide readily in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade with rich, moist soil.

directions: This heavily silvered selection carries a fine green vein pattern across near-metallic leaves. Site it in part to full shade with steady moisture and set the crown level with the soil surface. Airy blue flowers rise in spring; keep it out of hot afternoon sun and propagate by dividing established clumps.

planting.note: Surface-sow the fine seed under light in late winter and set plants out after frost.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto moist soil without covering it, as light aids germination. Grow in average well-drained ground and deadhead the tall wiry stems to prolong flowering. Cup-shaped blue or white bells open along the spikes from early to midsummer, usually in the second year from seed.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant with the crown level with the soil surface and stake in exposed sites. Remove spent flower spikes to encourage a second flush and divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous. Large lavender-blue bells appear on upright stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist but well-drained ground.

directions: Set plants at the same depth they grew in the pot and water in well. Deadhead regularly to extend the display and cut stems back after flowering. The white bells are delicately edged with soft blue and open through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; this species can bloom the first year from an early sowing.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface and keep it lightly moist and warm until sprouts appear. Grow in gritty, well-drained soil at the front of a border or in a rock garden. Upright open cups in blue or white cover the tidy mounds from early summer into autumn if deadheaded.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for compact plants that flower the first season.

directions: Surface-sow and provide light and warmth for even germination. Space the mounds along an edging in sharply drained soil and shear lightly after the first flush. Large upward-facing lavender-blue bells smother the foliage from early summer onward.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for first-year bloom on neat mounds.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and keep consistently moist until seedlings emerge. Set out in well-drained soil where the low cushions can spill over a path edge. Pure white open cups bloom heavily from early summer, repeating if spent flowers are removed.

planting.note: Usually planted from divisions or pots in spring into a wall crevice or rock garden.

directions: Tuck plants into gritty, sharply drained soil or between paving and wall stones. Trim lightly after the main flush to keep the mat neat and vigorous. Masses of star-shaped violet-blue bells cover the trailing foliage in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant from pots in spring in containers, baskets, or along a retaining wall.

directions: Position at the edge of a wall or basket so the stems can cascade freely. Water during dry spells and shear after flowering to encourage a rebloom. Sheets of star-shaped blue flowers spill downward from late spring well into summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow and keep warm and moist for germination, then set plants in fertile soil. Deadhead spent heads and divide clumps if they spread too freely. Dense clusters of upward-facing purple bells crown the stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile ground.

directions: Set plants in rich soil that stays evenly moist and give them room to colonize. Cut back after flowering to tidy the clump and possibly prompt a second flush. Bold clusters of violet-purple bells sit atop the stems in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant from divisions or pots in spring in fertile, moist soil.

directions: Plant into well-worked soil and mulch to retain moisture through summer. Remove faded heads to keep the plant looking fresh and prevent excess seeding. Rounded clusters of soft dusky-pink bells open in early summer above the leafy clumps.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; plants usually flower from the second year.

directions: Sow on the surface and grow on in deep, fertile soil that holds moisture. Stake the tall stems in windy gardens and cut back after the first flush for later blooms. Broad heads of small pale lilac-blue bells cover the plant through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in deep, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Set in rich soil and provide support for the tall flowering stems. Deadhead the main heads to draw out a longer season of bloom. Large branching sprays of soft lilac-pink bells rise in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant from divisions in spring in fertile, moist ground.

directions: Grow in deep soil enriched with compost and stake in exposed positions. Cut spent stems back to promote fresh side shoots and later flowers. Airy panicles of deep violet-blue bells appear across summer.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors in late winter and set out into lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface and keep just moist until germination. Grow in poor, sharply drained ground where this dainty native thrives on neglect. Nodding lavender-blue bells sway on thread-fine stems from early summer to autumn.

planting.note: Surface-sow and set the small plants into a trough or rock crevice.

directions: Sow the dust-fine seed on the surface and grow in gritty alpine soil with sharp drainage. The creeping mats spread slowly by runners and dislike waterlogging. Tiny nodding blue or white thimble bells appear in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into a trough or gritty rock garden pocket.

directions: Set the small plants into very well-drained alpine soil and top-dress with grit. Keep the creeping mat clear of heavy mulch that could rot the crowns. Double, powder-blue thimble bells dangle over the low foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Set plants in rich, evenly moist ground and give them room to spread. Deadhead spent stems to encourage more flowers over a longer season. Pendulous rose-red bells rimmed in white dangle from upright stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant from pots or divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Grow in humus-rich soil in part shade and keep steadily moist through summer. Its runners spread, so divide or edge it if you want to contain the clump. Unusual pink bells with long finger-like petals give a spidery look in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; spreads readily by underground runners.

directions: Surface-sow and grow the plants in cool, moist soil in part shade. This vigorous spreader colonizes freely, so plant it where it can roam or edge it in. Long pendulous cream-to-lilac bells spotted with maroon hang along arching stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich soil in part shade.

directions: Set plants in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and mulch to keep roots cool. It spreads by runners, so divide or contain it to manage its vigor. Large dusky rose-pink bells heavily spotted inside dangle from the stems in early summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or direct in a cool woodland-edge site.

directions: Surface-sow and grow in moist, fertile soil in dappled shade or sun. This robust species self-seeds gently and suits informal or wildlife plantings. Clusters of bell-shaped violet-blue flowers open up the stems in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Surface-sow and plant into a wall or rock garden after frost.

directions: Press the fine seed onto gritty soil and grow in sharp drainage with full sun. Ideal spilling from crevices in a dry wall or trough where it needs little care. Star-shaped blue flowers with pale centers cover the low mats through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into gritty, well-drained rock garden soil.

directions: Set into sharply drained soil and give a little afternoon shade to protect the bright foliage from scorch. The chartreuse-gold leaves make a striking mat even out of bloom. Starry blue flowers stud the golden cushions in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or pots in spring in fertile, moist soil.

directions: Set plants in rich, well-drained soil in sun or light shade and stake if needed. Deadhead the arching stems to prolong the display over several weeks. Large glossy deep violet-blue bells nod from the stems through summer.

planting.note: Plant from divisions in spring in moist but well-drained ground.

directions: Grow in fertile soil in sun or part shade and keep evenly moist in summer. This near-sterile hybrid stays put and reblooms if cut back after the first flush. Large pendulous deep violet-purple bells open over a long season in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into a rock garden, wall, or container edge.

directions: Set into gritty, well-drained soil where the trailing stems can spill over an edge. Shear lightly after the main flush to keep the mat compact and encourage rebloom. Deep violet-blue bells smother the spreading foliage from early to late summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and plant out in well-drained soil.

directions: Surface-sow and grow the heart-leaved plants in well-drained ordinary soil. Tolerant of dry shade and poor conditions once established, it needs little fuss. Arching stems carry nodding creamy-white bells through summer.

planting.note: Sow one summer for bloom the next; treat as a biennial in most gardens.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed as it needs light, and grow on a leafy rosette the first year. Overwinter the plants and set them in fertile soil for their flowering year. Tall spikes of large cup-and-saucer bells in blue, pink, or white open in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Sow in spring for tall spires the following year; often grown as a biennial.

directions: Surface-sow and grow on a strong rosette the first season in fertile, free-draining soil. Stake the towering flower spike, which appears in the plant's second year. Dozens of pale blue or white open bells line the tall pyramidal stems in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Surface-sow in late winter and grow this short-lived alpine in gritty soil.

directions: Press the fine seed onto gritty, sharply drained soil in a cool alpine spot. This short-lived species may self-sow to renew itself in suitable conditions. Nodding pale blue bells fringed with fine hairs open on slender stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich soil in part shade.

directions: Grow in fertile, evenly moist soil and give the runners room to spread. Divide or edge the clump if it wanders farther than you want. Distinctive double-skirted pink bells hang from the arching stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant from pots or divisions in spring in a well-drained container or border edge.

directions: Set into free-draining soil at the front of a bed or in a pot in sun or light shade. Deadhead spent flowers to keep the compact plant blooming for months. Upward-facing violet-blue bells with a white eye release a light fragrance through summer.

planting.note: Surface-sow in late winter and plant into lean, sharply drained soil.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface and keep barely moist until it sprouts. Grow in poor, gritty soil where it forms airy tufts and self-sows lightly. Nodding rich blue bells dance on wiry stems from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Very aggressive; plant only where its spreading roots can be contained.

directions: Grow in almost any soil, but be warned this species spreads relentlessly by seed and deep roots. Confine it to a container or a spot where its running habit is welcome. One-sided spikes of nodding violet-blue bells open up the stems in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; flowers from the second year in fertile soil.

directions: Sow on the surface and grow the seedlings on in deep, moisture-retentive soil. Support the tall stems and cut back after the first flush for later flowers. Broad heads of small white bells rise above the leafy clumps through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant into fertile, moist soil.

directions: Surface-sow and grow on before setting plants in rich, evenly moist ground. Remove faded heads and divide clumps that spread too far. Tight terminal clusters of pure white upward-facing bells open in early summer.

planting.note: Sow outdoors around the last spring frost or start indoors a few weeks earlier.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter inch deep in ordinary, well-drained soil and keep it lightly moist until sprouts appear. Cut plants back hard after the first flush to encourage a second round of the fringed blue blooms in late summer. Expect the earliest cornflower-blue flowers by late spring of the second season.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew before and firm the soil around the crown. Deadhead spent flowers to keep the clean white blooms coming and to limit self-sowing. Look for the first fringed white flowers in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to light shade with good drainage.

directions: Set plants out in spring and water them in well while they establish. Shear the clump back after flowering to tidy the foliage and prompt a light rebloom. The striking white petals with a purple central boss open from late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in average, freely draining soil.

directions: Plant at the original crown depth and mulch lightly to hold moisture. Remove faded flowers regularly to extend the display of deep amethyst-purple blooms. First flowers appear in late spring and continue into summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; give it a little afternoon shade in hot regions to protect the gold leaves.

directions: Site this golden-leaved form where it gets bright light but is shielded from scorching midday sun. Trim off spent blooms to show off the chartreuse-gold foliage that is the main attraction. Blue flowers rise above the bright leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the darkest flower color.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil and keep it on the lean side for sturdy stems. Deadhead often to draw out the run of near-black maroon flowers. The dusky blooms begin in late spring above green foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and average soil.

directions: Set plants out in spring and stake taller clumps if they flop in rich soil. Cut the whole plant back after the first bloom to renew the foliage and coax a second flush. Its extra-large blue flowers open from late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in freely draining soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Plant divisions at the crown depth they were growing before and water in gently. Snip off faded blooms to keep the soft rosy-pink flowers coming and reduce reseeding. First flowers appear in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and ordinary garden soil.

directions: Plant this clear-blue selection in spring and keep it evenly moist while it roots in. Deadhead through the season to prolong bloom and prevent volunteer seedlings. The vivid blue, spidery flowers start in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant in spring and avoid overly rich soil so stems stay upright. Remove spent flowers to encourage repeat bloom of the white petals surrounding a rich purple eye. Flowering opens in late spring and can rebloom with a midsummer shearing.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the deepest flower color.

directions: Set this compact form in lean, well-drained ground and keep it in strong light. Deadhead regularly to sustain the deep maroon, almost chocolate-toned flowers. Blooms open in late spring on tidy mounds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in average soil in sun or dappled shade.

directions: Plant at the same depth as the nursery pot and water in thoroughly. Cut back after the first flush to freshen foliage and prompt more bloom. The deep violet-purple flowers begin appearing in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant divisions in spring and firm the soil around the roots. Deadhead spent blooms to keep the rich amethyst-blue flowers arriving over a long spell. Flowering begins in late spring above the gray-green foliage.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost or set out plants in spring.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in well-drained soil and thin seedlings to give each plant room. Deadhead the fringed rose-pink flowers to prolong bloom and cut foliage back after flowering. Expect the first flowers by early summer of the second year.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Set plants out in spring and provide support if the clump grows lax in rich ground. Remove faded flowers to encourage a longer show of the deep carmine-pink, white-centered blooms. Flowering opens in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Plant in spring in average, well-drained soil and avoid wet winter sites. Cut plants back after the first flush of bloom to trigger a strong repeat later in the season. The fragrant, rosy-pink flowers with pale centers open from early summer.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost; this large plant needs room to spread.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep and thin to well-spaced plants, since mature clumps are broad and bold. Give this tall species full sun and support in exposed spots. The bright yellow thistle-like globes, prized for cutting and drying, open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost as an easygoing groundcover.

directions: Sow shallowly in lean, well-drained soil where its silvery mat can knit together. Trim after bloom to keep the low foliage tidy and dense. Soft lilac-pink flowers rise just above the carpet in early summer.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost; seed benefits from a cold spell before germinating.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and be patient, as germination can be slow and uneven. Give this stately plant full sun and deep, fertile soil for best stem height. The pale creamy-yellow flowers over glossy divided foliage open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin seedlings to stand a hand's width apart. This tidy species suits the front of a border where its silvery foliage stays neat. Bright blue cornflower blooms with a rosy center open in late spring.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost; give this tall species plenty of room.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep and thin to strong, well-spaced plants. Stake this tall grower in windy gardens and give it full sun. The deep wine-purple thistle flowers appear in mid to late summer above ferny gray foliage.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost in full sun and lean soil.

directions: Sow shallowly and expect slow, uneven germination from this drought-tough species. Once rooted it asks little beyond sun and sharp drainage. Soft pale-yellow flowers over finely cut foliage open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost as a low edging or rock-garden plant.

directions: Sow shallowly in gritty, fast-draining soil where the silvery mat can spread. Keep it lean and sunny and trim after bloom to keep the foliage compact. Soft pink flowers hover just above the frosted leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin seedlings to keep the mounds airy. This compact species combines silvery foliage with an early, tidy bloom. Rich rose-pink, fringed flowers open in late spring above the felted leaves.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost; a fine choice for meadows and pollinator plantings.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in lean, chalky or well-drained soil and thin to give plants room. This meadow native thrives on neglect once established and draws bees and butterflies. Large rose-purple thistle flowers open across mid to late summer.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost for wildflower meadows and naturalistic borders.

directions: Scatter seed on lean soil in autumn or spring and press it into contact with the ground. This tough meadow plant needs little care and is a magnet for bees and butterflies. Hard-headed reddish-purple flowers open from midsummer into autumn.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost in a sunny meadow or border.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in ordinary or lean soil and thin lightly if crowded. Leave the seed heads standing in autumn to feed birds and self-sow. Rosy-purple flowers with distinctive brown bracts open through summer.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost in sun to light shade.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in moist, well-drained soil and thin the seedlings. This species tolerates damper ground than most cornflowers and suits a wildflower planting. Purple-pink flowers backed by shaggy, feathery bracts open in summer.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost; grown mainly for its silver foliage.

directions: Start seed indoors and set plants out once frost has passed in fast-draining soil. Pinch out flower stems if you want to keep the plant focused on its felted silver-white leaves. The woolly foliage looks best in full sun and lean, dry conditions.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost as a low, mat-forming edger.

directions: Sow shallowly in gritty, well-drained soil where the low silvery mat can spread. Keep it lean and sunny and shear lightly after bloom. Soft creamy-yellow flowers open just above the foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost; give this tall plant space at the back of a border.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin to widely spaced plants for this large species. It thrives in full sun and lean soil and needs little water once settled. Bright golden-yellow flowers open along tall stems in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost; a compact species for rock gardens and troughs.

directions: Sow shallowly in gritty alpine soil and give it cool nights and sharp drainage. This mountain species stays compact and dislikes wet winter ground. A single fluffy rose-purple flower tops each stem in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow or set out plants after frost danger passes in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: This pure-white form is grown the same as the species, needing only lean soil, sun, and good drainage to flourish. Surface-sow seed or space nursery plants a foot and a half apart, watering sparingly once established. Deadhead the ivory clusters to keep bloom going into late summer and to curb seeding.

planting.note: Set out or direct-sow after the last spring frost in full sun.

directions: Selected for its richer carmine-red flowers, this form is otherwise the tough, drought-loving valerian of dry walls and gravel. Plant in gritty, alkaline soil with unobstructed sun and avoid rich or wet ground that causes floppy growth. Cut back hard after the main flush to encourage a fresh crop of blooms and tidier clumps.

planting.note: Sow outdoors or transplant seedlings once frosts have finished.

directions: A soft rose-pink selection prized for cottage borders and self-seeding into stony crevices. Give it full sun and free-draining, limey soil, and water only during prolonged drought. Shear the faded stems to prompt repeat flowering and to keep volunteer seedlings in check.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in poor, sharply drained soil.

directions: The darkest of the red valerians, with deep crimson flower heads over blue-green foliage. Site it in blazing sun and lean, gravelly ground where it will bloom for months on little water. Remove spent heads to extend the display and reduce its prolific reseeding.

planting.note: Start indoors several weeks before last frost for first-year bloom.

directions: A seed-raised white strain that flowers reliably in its first year when sown early indoors. Surface-sow and provide light and warmth to germinate, then harden off and set out after frost in sunny, well-drained soil. Deadhead the airy white clusters to keep the plant blooming and compact through summer.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in a hot, dry, alkaline site.

directions: This southern European valerian carries dense heads of pale lilac to mauve flowers over glaucous foliage. It relishes the same baking sun and stony, limey soil as red valerian and shrugs off heat and drought. Trim back after flowering to encourage a second flush and to keep it from seeding freely.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, sharply drained ground in full sun.

directions: Distinguished by slender grassy leaves and soft pink flower clusters, this species suits rock gardens and dry walls. Give it lean, alkaline soil and full exposure, watering only in extended dry spells. Cut back spent stems to tidy the clump and prompt further bloom into late summer.

planting.note: Direct-sow after frost where plants are to flower.

directions: A fast, showy annual valerian bearing rounded heads of long-spurred pink flowers in a single season. Sow seed directly into warm, well-drained soil after frost and thin seedlings to give each room to branch. Deadhead regularly for continuous bloom, and let a few heads ripen if you want it to self-sow for next year.

planting.note: Scatter-sow in spring into lean, well-drained soil.

directions: A small Mediterranean annual with divided leaves and loose sprays of tiny pale pink to whitish flowers. Direct-sow onto dry, gravelly ground in spring and expect quick, undemanding growth in full sun. It flowers within a couple of months and often self-seeds into cracks and poor soil.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or set out young plants once the ground has warmed in spring.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and press it into gritty, fast-draining soil, keeping it lightly moist until sprouts appear. Give plenty of sun and lean, dry ground, then shear the silvery mat hard after flowering to keep it tidy and stop it wandering. Expect a sheet of white star-shaped blooms over the gray foliage in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant into sharply drained soil in spring; this compact form is well suited to rock crevices and edging.

directions: Sow shallowly in gritty mix and keep barely damp until germination, then move seedlings to a hot, dry spot. This tighter, lower-growing selection stays neater than the common species but still benefits from a shearing once bloom fades. Look for its carpet of white flowers over felted silver leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors or set plants out in spring into lean, well-drained ground.

directions: Surface-sow onto free-draining soil and keep lightly moist under bright light until the seedlings appear. This more restrained, uniform selection makes a dense low mound that is easier to keep in bounds than the wild species. Shear after the flush of white spring blooms to promote a compact, silvery habit.

planting.note: Set divisions or rooted pieces into gritty soil in spring so they establish before summer heat.

directions: Lift and pull apart an established clump in spring and replant the rooted sections at the same depth in dry, sunny ground. Prized for an especially bright silver carpet of foliage, it spreads steadily and roots along the stems. Trim back after the white flowers finish to keep the mat dense and even.

planting.note: Give it room to run; plant in spring into poor, dry soil where its spread is welcome.

directions: Press the small seed onto gritty soil, cover lightly, and keep just moist until germination. Similar to common snow-in-summer but with broader, whiter leaves and slightly larger flowers, it can spread vigorously, so site it where it can roam or contain it with edging. Cut back after the late-spring bloom to renew the silver mat.

planting.note: Set into a sharply drained scree or trough in spring; this true alpine dislikes wet, heavy soil.

directions: Sow into a gritty alpine mix and expose seed to a period of cold to break dormancy before warmth triggers germination. This compact, cushion-forming species needs perfect drainage and cool conditions, making it a rock-garden or trough plant rather than a border groundcover. Enjoy its relatively large white flowers over woolly gray tufts in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant in a very free-draining trough or scree bed in spring and protect the woolly foliage from winter wet.

directions: Give the seed a cold spell before moving it to gentle warmth in gritty compost. This densely white-woolly form of the alpine mouse-ear forms neat silvery cushions and demands sharp drainage and airflow to avoid rot. White star flowers rise just above the felted mat in early summer.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in spring into lean, well-drained soil; tolerant of poor, dry sites.

directions: Scatter seed on prepared ground and rake in lightly, keeping the surface damp until seedlings establish. This tough native forms low, spreading tufts of green-gray leaves and is well suited to meadows, banks, and wildflower plantings. It carries loose sprays of white notched-petal flowers through late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Set out in spring into gritty, sunny ground; the tidy habit suits rock gardens and edging.

directions: Start seed shallowly in a free-draining mix and keep evenly moist until germination, then grow on in bright light. This dwarf selection stays denser and more mounded than the wild field chickweed, making a neat cushion for the rock garden. It is topped with white star flowers in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring into hot, sharply drained soil; this Mediterranean species loves sun and dry summers.

directions: Sow onto gritty compost, cover lightly, and keep just moist under warmth until seedlings show. Native to the mountains of Greece, it forms an intensely silver-white woolly mat and appreciates lean soil and excellent drainage. White flowers appear a little later than common snow-in-summer, into early summer.

planting.note: Set into a sunny scree or raised bed in spring where drainage is fast and summers run dry.

directions: Sow in a gritty mix and keep barely damp in good light until the small seedlings appear. This Iberian species forms tight silvery cushions and is grown for its bright foliage and neat form in rock and gravel gardens. Loose clusters of white flowers open above the mat in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Sow stratified seed or divide established clumps in spring.

directions: Give seed a cold-moist period, then press it onto the surface of damp seed-starting mix and hold at room temperature. Plant in humus-rich soil that never dries out and divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous. The rosy-red to deep-pink hooded flowers appear in late summer and draw bumblebees that pry the lips open.

planting.note: Sow chilled seed in spring; this uncommon native prefers boggy ground.

directions: Cold-stratify the seed and surface-sow it on wet mix, as this species is happiest in permanently saturated soil. Grow it in a bog garden or seep with its more rounded, purple-tinged pink flowers, and avoid letting the roots dry. Blooms come in late summer and hold into early autumn.

planting.note: Set out potted plants or divisions in spring so roots establish before bloom.

directions: Plant this vegetatively propagated selection at the same depth it grew in the pot and mulch to hold moisture. Prized for red-flushed stems and bronzy young foliage, it wants rich, wet soil and takes full sun where roots stay damp. Deep rose-pink flowers top the dark stems from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions or containers in spring in consistently moist ground.

directions: Install this named selection in fertile, damp soil and keep it evenly watered through summer heat. A sturdy, upright form, it holds its stems well without staking in part shade to sun. Soft pink hooded flowers cluster at the stem tips from late summer into early fall.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant this white-flowered form at its original depth and mulch to conserve soil moisture. It performs like the species, thriving beside ponds and in rain gardens where it never dries out. Clean white turtle-head blooms open from late summer through early fall.

planting.note: Set out divisions or sow seed after the soil warms in spring.

directions: Space plants so their airy, needle-like foliage can knit into a soft mound, and give them lean, well-drained ground. They tolerate heat and drought once rooted and rarely need staking. Shear the whole clump back by a third after the first flush to trigger a second wave of bloom.

planting.note: Plant nursery divisions in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Set divisions at the same depth they grew in the pot and water in well while roots settle. This sterile selection blooms nonstop with pale butter-yellow flowers and needs almost no fuss in lean soil. A light midsummer shearing keeps the mound tidy and the flowers flowing into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the ground warms.

directions: Plant this compact selection in full sun and lean, sharply drained soil for the sturdiest habit. Its golden-yellow flowers cover the fine foliage all summer with little care. Cut the clump back after peak bloom to refresh the mound and coax more flowers.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost; it also self-seeds readily.

directions: Scatter seed on prepared ground and rake lightly, since it germinates fast in warm soil. This quick annual bears masses of yellow flowers marked with mahogany-red centers and thrives on neglect. Leave some seed heads at season's end and it will reseed itself for the following year.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist ground, unlike its drought-loving cousins.

directions: Unlike most tickseeds this species likes consistently moist soil, so keep it watered during dry spells. Its thread-fine foliage carries small rose-pink daisies with yellow eyes through summer. Shear lightly after the first flush and it will spread gently to form a soft colony.

planting.note: Sow shallowly in spring; often blooms the first year from an early start.

directions: Barely cover the seed since light aids germination, and thin seedlings once they have a few true leaves. This short-lived perennial rewards a first-year sowing with big golden daisies on wiry stems. Deadhead often to prolong bloom and let a few heads mature if you want volunteer seedlings.

planting.note: Start indoors six weeks early to enjoy first-year bloom.

directions: Sow on the surface under lights and keep warm for quick, even germination. This award-winning strain bears semi-double golden-yellow flowers on compact plants that bloom the first summer. Snip off faded blooms regularly and the plants will flower for months.

planting.note: Start seed indoors for double flowers the first season.

directions: Press seed into the surface and keep evenly moist until sprouts appear. This selection produces fully double, bright golden flowers that look like little pom-poms and last well when cut. Remove spent blooms to keep new buds forming through the season.

planting.note: Plant nursery divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Give this variegated selection full sun and sharp drainage for the best leaf color. Its foliage is edged in cream and pink while the golden flowers glow above. Deadhead through summer and protect from soggy winter soil, which it dislikes.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring once the soil warms.

directions: Plant this taller threadleaf type where its fine foliage can form a rounded clump. It bears bright golden-yellow star-shaped flowers all summer and asks only for sun and good drainage. Shear the mound after the main flush to keep it neat and blooming.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates a bit more shade than most tickseeds.

directions: This low woodland-edge species spreads by short runners to form a modest groundcover. It takes part shade and slightly richer soil than sun-loving tickseeds. Cut back after the spring flush of orange-yellow daisies to encourage repeat blooms.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for a low edging or groundcover.

directions: Tuck this dwarf selection along a path edge where its low rosettes can slowly knit together. It flowers in late spring with cheerful golden-orange daisies on short stems. Give it even moisture and light shade in hot climates, then shear after bloom.

planting.note: Sow in spring or fall; benefits from cold to break dormancy.

directions: Give this towering native plenty of room at the back of a border or meadow. It blooms in late summer with pale yellow daisies held high on strong stems that rarely need staking. The foliage smells faintly of anise when brushed, and pollinators flock to the flowers.

planting.note: Sow in fall or cold-stratify seed before a spring sowing.

directions: This tough prairie native spreads by rhizomes to form a durable stand in lean, dry soil. Its stiff three-lobed leaves and pale yellow flowers thrive without irrigation once rooted. Sow in fall so winter cold prepares the seed to sprout the following spring.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost for a compact annual show.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the surface and press it in gently, since light speeds sprouting. This dwarf strain produces deep mahogany-red flowers on tidy compact plants ideal for the front of a bed. It blooms fast from seed and self-sows for a return the next year.

planting.note: Plant nursery divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Set this hybrid in full sun and well-drained soil for the sturdiest plants. Its golden flowers have distinctive fluted, tube-shaped petals that give the blooms a fringed look. Deadhead through summer and shear lightly if the clump begins to sprawl.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Give this selection full sun and lean soil to show off its two-toned blooms. Each yellow flower carries a spreading red blotch that grows larger as the season cools. Remove faded flowers to keep the plant tidy and flowering into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; hardiness is marginal, so treat as tender in cold zones.

directions: Plant this fine-leaved hybrid in full sun with excellent drainage. It bears rich ruby-red flowers over threadlike foliage all summer long. It is only reliably winter-hardy in mild regions, so grow it as an annual or overwinter it where frosts are hard.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Give this spreading hybrid room, as it forms a broad low mound of foliage. Its creamy-white flowers carry a small burgundy eye and cover the plant for months. Shear back after the first heavy flush to keep the blooms coming.

planting.note: Plant in spring for late-season color that deepens with cool weather.

directions: Plant this vigorous hybrid where it has room to build a large mound of fine foliage. Its pale yellow flowers develop deepening red centers as nights turn cool in autumn. Cut it back hard in late spring if you want a bushier, more compact plant.

planting.note: Plant nursery divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Site this Big Bang series hybrid in full sun with good drainage and space to spread. It bears unusually large canary-yellow flowers on strong stems through summer. Deadhead or shear lightly to keep the display fresh into early fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once the ground warms.

directions: Plant this compact hybrid in full sun for the brightest two-toned flowers. Each bloom is deep red with a bold golden-yellow edge, giving a bullseye effect. Trim off spent blooms regularly and it will flower steadily through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost for a hardy, wine-red display.

directions: Set this hardy hybrid in full sun and free-draining soil. It bears velvety wine-red flowers with a small gold eye over a compact mound. It is more cold-tolerant than many red tickseeds, but still appreciates dry feet in winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Give this compact hybrid full sun and it will form a dense mound of oversized flowers. Each bloom is red at the center with a broad yellow rim, held on tidy well-branched stems. Its neat habit rarely needs shearing, though deadheading extends the show.

planting.note: Sow in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing.

directions: This upright native of open woods and clearings tolerates poor, dry soil. Its whorled leaves and yellow daisies bloom in early to midsummer and draw many pollinators. Sow seed in fall so winter cold readies it to germinate in spring.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost for a dwarf annual.

directions: Sow the tiny seed on the surface and firm gently for good contact. This compact annual bears golden-yellow flowers with a rich maroon central zone. It blooms quickly from seed and reseeds itself for an easy return next season.

planting.note: Sow in spring or fall; adapts to a range of soils.

directions: This adaptable native takes both dry and moist soils and a little shade. Its softly hairy leaves and golden daisies bloom over a long summer season. Deadhead or shear midway through summer to keep the plant flowering and compact.

planting.note: Start indoors early for first-year flowers.

directions: Surface-sow and keep warm and bright for prompt germination. This selection bears golden flowers with a bold red ring around the eye. Remove spent blooms often and the plants keep producing through the summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Give this Permathread-type hybrid full sun and lean, well-drained ground. Its fine threadlike foliage carries bright orange-red flowers all summer. The tough stems resist flopping, and a light shearing renews the display.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for a dwarf front-of-border plant.

directions: Set this dwarf Li'l Bang series hybrid at the front of a sunny bed or in a container. Its golden-yellow flowers each carry a deep red eye and cover the compact mound. It stays tidy without shearing, though removing old blooms keeps it fresh.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Plant this double-flowered selection in full sun with good drainage. Its fully double golden blooms form rounded balls that stand out in a border or bouquet. Deadhead spent flowers to keep new buds coming through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors for first-year bloom or sow outside in spring.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it lit, since light helps it sprout. This compact strain bears single golden flowers with a small red-brown center. It flowers the first year from an early sowing and keeps blooming when deadheaded.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once the soil warms.

directions: Plant this larger-flowered threadleaf type in full sun and lean soil. Its bright golden star-shaped flowers are bigger than typical threadleaf types and cover the airy foliage. Shear the whole clump after the main flush to renew the bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost passes.

directions: Set this hybrid in full sun for the richest flower color. Its blooms open a warm burnt-orange and mellow to peach and amber as they age. Deadhead through summer to keep the plant tidy and flowering.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Give this threadleaf selection full sun and free-draining soil. Its large soft-yellow flowers cover a rounded mound of fine foliage all summer. Shear the plant back after the first big flush for a strong repeat performance.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once the soil warms.

directions: Plant this threadleaf type in full sun and lean, well-drained ground. It produces abundant deep golden-yellow flowers over finely cut foliage. Shear after the main bloom to keep the mound neat and encourage more flowers.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Plant this hybrid in full sun for the deepest flower color. Its blooms blend raspberry-pink and coral tones that shift as they mature. Deadhead through the season to keep the compact mound flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist ground; blooms very late in the season.

directions: This rare southeastern native likes constantly moist to wet soil at a stream edge or rain garden. It spreads by rhizomes and bursts into golden bloom in mid to late autumn when little else flowers. Give it steady moisture and it will form a slow-spreading colony.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant this upright hybrid in full sun and well-drained soil. It bears bright golden daisies over narrow willow-like leaves through summer. Deadhead or shear lightly to keep the plant blooming and prevent flopping.

planting.note: Start seed indoors for first-year, dwarf flowering.

directions: Surface-sow and keep bright and warm for quick sprouting. This dwarf strain forms tidy mounds topped with semi-double golden flowers the first year. Its compact size suits the front of a border or a container, and deadheading extends bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Give this compact hybrid full sun and free-draining soil. Its golden-yellow flowers each carry a soft red central ring on a tidy mound. Remove faded blooms to keep the plant flowering steadily through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant this compact hybrid in full sun and lean soil. Its white flowers carry a bright raspberry-red center that gives a striking two-tone effect. Deadhead through the season to keep the small mound covered in bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Unlike drought-loving tickseeds, this selection prefers evenly moist soil. Its fine foliage is covered with rich pink daisies that have small golden eyes. Shear lightly after the first flush and keep it watered for a long bloom season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist ground.

directions: Give this pink tickseed the steady moisture it prefers rather than letting it dry out. Its threadlike foliage is smothered in deep rose-pink flowers with darker centers. Trim lightly after the main flush to refresh the plant and prolong bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost passes.

directions: Set this very compact hybrid at the front of a bed or in a container in full sun. Its small daisies open a rich rose-red with a golden eye over neat foliage. Its tidy size needs no shearing, though deadheading keeps it flowering.

planting.note: Sow in spring in moist soil; blooms in fall in warm regions.

directions: This Florida-native tickseed likes damp, sandy soil at a pond edge or wet meadow. It sends up bright golden daisies on slender stems in late summer and fall. Keep the soil from drying out and it will bloom reliably in mild-winter gardens.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and set young plants out after the last hard frost.

directions: Barely cover the tiny seed and keep the flat cool and evenly moist until sprouts appear in two to four weeks. Set plants in rich, well-drained soil and stake the tall flower spikes before they reach knee height. The first blue candles open in early summer; cut them back to encourage a lighter fall flush.

planting.note: Start indoors in early spring; this compact species often blooms its first year.

directions: Press seed lightly into a cool, moist flat and expect sprouts within about three weeks. This airy, branching species stays short enough to skip staking and tolerates heat better than the tall hybrids. Deep blue, loosely spaced flowers appear in early summer and repeat if spent stems are trimmed.

planting.note: Usually grown from divisions or potted plants set out in spring.

directions: Plant potted divisions in fertile, well-drained soil with the crown at ground level. Unlike the heavy Pacific hybrids, these branching plants carry loose sprays that rarely need heavy staking. Airy blue flowers open in early summer and rebloom in fall when the first stems are cut down.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for towering spikes the following summer.

directions: Sow in a chilled, moist flat and barely cover the seed, keeping it out of direct heat. Feed generously and stake each dense spike early because the large double flowers catch wind and rain. This mixed strain blooms in a full range of blues, purples, pinks and white in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant out once frost danger passes.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the flat cool until germination. Enrich the bed with compost and stake the heavy spikes before bloom. Deep violet flowers with dark centers open in early summer atop towering stems.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for bloom the following summer.

directions: Sow in a cool, moist flat with only a dusting of soil over the seed. Give plants rich earth and stake the flower stems as they lengthen. Clear medium-blue florets with white central bees fill the spikes in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and set out after frost.

directions: Chill the moist flat and barely cover the seed until sprouts show. Plant in fertile soil and stake early, since the pure-white spikes are heavy when wet. Large snow-white double flowers with matching bees crown the plants in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for tall spikes next summer.

directions: Sow into a cool, damp flat and keep the seed barely covered. Amend the bed richly and stake the spikes before they flop. Royal violet-purple flowers set off by white bees appear in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed in a cool, moist flat and wait two to four weeks for sprouts. Grow in rich soil and stake the tall stems as buds form. Soft sky-blue flowers with white centers open in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for bloom the next summer.

directions: Sow in a chilled, moist flat with the seed barely covered. Feed well and stake early to protect the tall stems from wind. Uncommon lavender-pink flowers with dark bees fill the spikes in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and set out once frost has passed.

directions: Keep the seed flat cool and moist, barely covering the seed until it sprouts. Plant in compost-rich soil and stake the spikes before flowering. Lavender-pink petals brushed with mauve and set off by white bees open in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for tall summer spikes.

directions: Sow thinly in a cool, moist flat and barely cover the seed. Enrich the bed and stake the heavy stems early in the season. Clear medium-blue flowers marked with dark central bees open in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in early spring or set out potted plants after frost.

directions: Barely cover seed in a cool flat and keep it evenly moist until sprouts appear. These branching plants carry loose sprays and need only light staking. Deep gentian-blue single flowers open in early summer and repeat after a midseason cutback.

planting.note: Start indoors in early spring or transplant potted plants after frost.

directions: Keep the seed flat cool and moist and barely cover the seed. Plant in well-drained soil where the airy stems get gentle support. Pure-white single flowers glow above the foliage in early summer and rebloom if deadheaded.

planting.note: Best grown from cuttings or divisions set out in spring.

directions: Set young plants in rich, well-drained soil with the crown at the surface. The slender branching stems carry flowers loosely and need only light support. Soft sky-blue single blooms open in early summer and return in fall after a cutback.

planting.note: Start indoors in early spring for first-year bloom on this dwarf type.

directions: Press the seed lightly into a moist flat and keep it cool during germination. This compact, mounding selection needs no staking and often flowers its first summer. Vivid deep-blue flowers cover the plant for weeks and rebloom if trimmed.

planting.note: Start indoors in early spring; this dwarf blooms the first year.

directions: Barely press seed into a cool, moist flat and expect sprouts in about three weeks. The tidy, branching mound skips staking and handles summer heat well. Rich deep-blue flowers appear from early summer and repeat after shearing.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for compact spikes that often flower year one.

directions: Barely cover seed in a cool, moist flat and keep it out of heat. This shorter strain is bred for wind resistance and usually needs no staking. Dense dark-blue spikes with dark bees open in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for compact, self-supporting spikes.

directions: Keep the flat cool and moist, barely covering the seed until it sprouts. This dwarf strain resists wind and rarely needs support. Light sky-blue flowers with white bees fill the spikes in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for a shorter, sturdier spike.

directions: Barely cover seed in a cool flat and keep it evenly moist. This compact strain holds up in wind without heavy staking. Soft rose-pink flowers with white bees open in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for compact white spikes.

directions: Sow into a cool, moist flat with just a dusting of soil over the seed. The dwarf habit resists wind and usually stands without stakes. Clean white flowers with white bees crown the plants in early summer.

scientific_name: Delphinium 'Magic Fountains Lilac Pink White Bee'

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for sturdy dwarf spikes.

directions: Keep the seed flat cool and moist and barely cover the seed. This compact strain rarely needs staking in the border. Lilac-pink flowers with contrasting white bees open in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; this seed strain flowers uniformly the first year.

directions: Barely cover seed in a cool, moist flat and keep it shaded until sprouts appear. Bred for uniformity and cut-flower use, the spikes are strong but benefit from a single stake. Rich dark-blue flowers open in early summer along tight, well-filled stems.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for uniform first-year spikes.

directions: Keep the flat cool and barely cover the seed until germination. This tidy, floriferous strain makes an excellent cut flower and needs only light support. Soft lavender flowers fill the spikes in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for uniform white spikes.

directions: Barely cover seed in a cool, moist flat and keep it out of direct sun. The uniform, sturdy stems make fine cut flowers with light staking. Pure-white blooms crowd the spikes in early summer.

planting.note: A California native; start indoors after a cool, moist stratification.

directions: Chill the moist seed for a few weeks before sowing to break dormancy, then keep the flat cool. This drought-adapted native wants sharp drainage and dry summer rest after bloom. Spikes of vivid scarlet-red flowers open in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: A western native; cold-stratify seed before starting indoors.

directions: Give the seed several weeks of cool, moist chilling to improve germination. This slender native prefers gritty, well-drained soil and light shade in hot climates, going dormant after flowering. Loose sprays of orange-red tubular flowers appear in late spring.

planting.note: An eastern woodland native; sow fresh seed outdoors in fall for cold treatment.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall so winter cold breaks its deep dormancy, and be patient since sprouting can be slow. This spring ephemeral of rich woodlands goes dormant by midsummer after flowering. Short spikes of blue to violet flowers open among the leaf litter in early spring.

planting.note: A native meadow species; sow in fall for natural cold stratification.

directions: Sow seed in fall so winter cold triggers spring germination, or chill it artificially before an indoor start. This upright native of open woods and meadows tolerates part shade and average soil. Slender spikes of blue-violet flowers open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in early spring for first-year bloom on this dwarf.

directions: Press seed lightly into a moist flat and keep it cool until sprouting. This very compact selection is spurless with cupped flowers and needs no support. Intense gentian-blue flowers cover the low mounds in early summer.

planting.note: A New Millennium hybrid; start indoors in late winter for strong spikes.

directions: Barely cover seed in a cool, moist flat and keep it shaded until germination. This New Zealand-bred strain is unusually vigorous and heat-tolerant but still benefits from staking. Dense spikes of deep double purple flowers open in early summer.

planting.note: A New Millennium hybrid; start indoors in late winter.

directions: Sow into a cool, moist flat with the seed barely covered. Grow in rich soil and stake the tall spikes as buds swell. Sky-blue flowers flushed with soft pink and rose open in early summer.

planting.note: A New Millennium hybrid; start indoors in late winter for tall spikes.

directions: Keep the seed flat cool and moist and barely cover the seed. This robust strain resists heat better than older hybrids but still needs staking. Rose-pink flowers with white bees fill the tall spikes in early summer.

planting.note: A New Millennium hybrid; start indoors in late winter.

directions: Barely cover seed in a cool, moist flat and keep it out of heat until sprouting. Feed the vigorous plants well and stake before the spikes lengthen. Deep cobalt-blue flowers with contrasting bees open in early summer.

planting.note: A New Millennium hybrid; start indoors in late winter.

directions: Sow in a cool, moist flat with the seed barely covered. This heat-tolerant strain is vigorous but benefits from a stake in exposed sites. Soft dusky rose-mauve flowers with dark bees open in early summer.

planting.note: A New Millennium hybrid; start indoors in late winter.

directions: Barely cover the seed in a cool, moist flat and keep it shaded until it sprouts. Grow in fertile soil and stake the spikes early. Unusual white flowers brushed with soft green open in early summer.

planting.note: A Chinese species; start indoors in early spring for first-year bloom.

directions: Press seed lightly into a cool, moist flat and keep it evenly damp until sprouts show. This airy, wiry-stemmed species branches freely and needs no staking. Small spurred flowers of clear cornflower-blue dance above the foliage in summer.

planting.note: A short-lived Mediterranean species; sow where it is to grow and let it self-seed.

directions: Sow seed in place in a warm, well-drained spot since this species dislikes being moved. Grown as a biennial, it forms glossy rosettes the first year and flowers the next before setting seed. Branching spikes of small pale lavender-blue flowers with dark eyes open in early summer.

planting.note: A tuberous Central Asian species; start indoors and give it a dry summer rest.

directions: Sow into a cool, well-drained flat and keep moisture modest, as this dryland species resents wet feet. It grows from a tuber, blooms, then dies back for a dry dormant period, so hold off watering after flowering. Slender spikes of rare pure-yellow flowers open in summer.

planting.note: A named English hybrid usually propagated by cuttings or division.

directions: Set young plants in deep, fertile soil and stake the emerging spikes well before bloom. This exhibition hybrid holds its color best with some afternoon shade in hot regions. Mid-blue semi-double flowers with white bees open in early summer and may repeat after cutback.

planting.note: A named hybrid propagated from cuttings or divisions set out in spring.

directions: Plant divisions in rich, well-drained soil and stake the spikes as they rise. One of the rare soft-yellow delphiniums, it shows best color with light shade from midday sun. Creamy pale-yellow flowers open in early summer along tall stems.

planting.note: A named exhibition hybrid grown from cuttings or divisions.

directions: Set plants in deep, fertile soil and stake the tall spikes early to guard against wind. This show-type hybrid produces densely packed double flowers that are heavy when wet. Bright cornflower-blue blooms with white bees open in early summer.

planting.note: A named exhibition hybrid propagated by cuttings or division.

directions: Plant in enriched, well-drained soil and stake the towering spikes before they bloom. This classic tall hybrid needs steady moisture and a sheltered spot to reach full height. Deep ultramarine-blue flowers with indigo overtones and dark bees open in early summer.

planting.note: A named hybrid grown from cuttings or divisions set out in spring.

directions: Set divisions in deep, fertile soil and support the spikes with stakes before flowering. This reliable garden hybrid holds up well and makes a strong border presence. Soft mid-blue flushed with mauve and set off by white bees opens in early summer.

planting.note: A named exhibition hybrid propagated from cuttings or division.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil and stake the tall spikes early in the season. This show-quality hybrid carries densely packed florets that benefit from a sheltered site. Lavender-blue flowers with striking white bees open in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and set out after the last hard frost.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and grow on in bright, cool conditions before transplanting into gritty, alkaline soil. Pinch young stems once to build bushier plants and stake tall flowering shoots against wind. Cut the double, richly perfumed blooms when the petals are half open for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring once the soil has warmed and settled.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on well-drained ground and press it in lightly rather than burying it. The low, spreading mats knit together quickly and throw up airy stems of small jewel-toned flowers in early summer. Shear lightly after the main flush to keep the foliage neat and coax a second show.

planting.note: Sow in spring, or take cuttings in summer to keep favorite forms true.

directions: Sow just below the surface into sharply drained, limey soil and thin the seedlings to give each room. The blue-grey grassy tufts carry fringed, spicy-scented flowers on wiry stems in early summer. Deadhead regularly and trim after flowering to keep the mound compact and long-lived.

planting.note: Sow in spring; plants may bloom lightly the first year and fully the next.

directions: Press the seed onto gritty, alkaline soil and keep barely moist until the tight blue-grey mat forms. Fragrant single flowers rise just above the cushion in late spring and early summer. Shear the whole clump after bloom to keep it dense and to invite a second scattering of flowers.

planting.note: Sow in spring in a moisture-retentive but drained spot.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the ground cool and evenly damp during germination. Tall, slender stems carry deeply and delicately fringed, sweetly scented flowers in summer. Cut back after the main flush and allow a few seed heads to ripen for self-sowing.

planting.note: Sow in a cold frame in late winter, as a chill helps germination.

directions: Surface-sow into gritty, free-draining mix and give the pots a cold spell to break dormancy. The tiny glossy-leaved cushions carry surprisingly large flowers close to the ground in early summer. Grow in a rock garden or trough with sharp drainage and avoid winter wet at the crown.
